The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.

2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean
4	
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion

8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	
10	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The minimal chain has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
32	Mumford, Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- it uses neither the
33	theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is the entry point for formalisation. The
34	\emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters only as the deep,
35	characteristic-free input that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb
36	P^1$ (the single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition~3.10); it is recorded here as a
37	\textbf{deferred} prerequisite with no formal proof yet. Combining the two gives that every
38	morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing
39	with the classification of genus-$0$ curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1},
40	$C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the headline
41	\cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain invokes neither Serre duality nor the FGA
42	$\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully decoupled from Route~A.
43	
44	\medskip
45	
46	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
47	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
48	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
49	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.

51	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
52	
53	\begin{lemma}
54	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
55	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
56	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
57	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
58	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
59	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
60	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
61	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
62	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
63	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
64	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
65	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
66	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
67	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
68	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
69	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
70	\end{lemma}
71	
72	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
73	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
74	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
75	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
76	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
77	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
78	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
79	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
80	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
81	% assertion."
82	\begin{proof}
83	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
84	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
85	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
86	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
87	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
88	
89	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
90	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
91	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
92	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
93	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
94	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
95	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
96	
97	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
98	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
99	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
100	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
101	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
102	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
103	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
104	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
105	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
106	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
107	\end{proof}
108	
109	\begin{remark}
110	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
111	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of $X$ makes
112	  $p_2$ a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
113	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
114	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
115	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
116	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
117	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically
118	  irreducible.)
119	\end{remark}

121	\section{The theorem of the cube (deferred deep input)}
122	
123	\textbf{Deferred prerequisite.} The block below records the theorem of the cube as the heavy,
124	\emph{not-yet-built} characteristic-free input that the single-curve base case of
125	\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} ultimately rests on. We give Mumford's statement
126	verbatim but \emph{no} formal proof: in Mumford the proof spans \S6 (the splitting
127	$T(X_0 \times \cdots \times X_n) = \operatorname{Im}\alpha \oplus \operatorname{Ker}\beta$
128	exhibiting $\Pic$ as a quadratic functor, then the seesaw principle), and the
129	characteristic-free scheme-theoretic form is Chapter~III \S10. This is the one genuinely
130	deep, un-formalised prerequisite of route~(c); it carries no Lean target yet.
131	
132	\begin{theorem}
133	[Theorem of the cube]
134	  \label{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
135	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Theorem (of the cube), Ch.~II \S6 ``The theorem of the cube: I'', book p.~55 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 66)
136	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM. Let $X$, $Y$ be complete varieties, $Z$ any variety and $x_0$,
137	  % $y_0$ and $z_0$ base points on $X$, $Y$, and $Z$, respectively. If $L$ is any line bundle
138	  % on $X \times Y \times Z$ whose restrictions to each of $\{x_0\} \times Y \times Z$,
139	  % $X \times \{y_0\} \times Z$ and $X \times Y \times \{z_0\}$ are trivial, $L$ is trivial."
140	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S6, Theorem of the cube, p.~55.}
141	  Let $X$, $Y$ be complete varieties and $Z$ any variety over $\bar k$, with base points
142	  $x_0 \in X$, $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$. If $L$ is a line bundle on $X \times Y \times Z$
143	  whose restrictions to each of $\{x_0\} \times Y \times Z$, $X \times \{y_0\} \times Z$, and
144	  $X \times Y \times \{z_0\}$ are trivial, then $L$ is trivial. \emph{(Recorded as a deferred
145	  input; no formal proof in this project yet --- this is the heavy un-built prerequisite of
146	  route~(c).)}
147	\end{theorem}

149	\section{Morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety are constant}
150	
151	\begin{proposition}
152	[A morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant]
153	  \label{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}
154	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const}
155	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
156	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.10 (specialised to a single $\mathbb P^1$ factor), \S I.3, doc p.~20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 26)
157	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.10. Every rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$
158	  % from a unirational variety to an abelian variety is constant."
159	  % SOURCE QUOTE (unirational, same page): "A variety $V$ over an algebraically closed field
160	  % is said to be unirational if there is a dominating rational map $\mathbb A^n
161	  % \dashrightarrow V$ with $n = \dim V$; equivalently, if $k(V)$ can be embedded into
162	  % $k(X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ (pure transcendental extension of $k$). A variety $V$ over an
163	  % arbitrary field $k$ is said to be unirational if $V_{k^{al}}$ is unirational."
164	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.10 (case $V = \mathbb P^1$).}
165	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $A$ an abelian variety over $\bar k$. Then every
166	  morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant: $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$ is a
167	  single $\bar k$-point.
168	\end{proposition}
169	
170	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. We may suppose that $k$ is algebraically closed. By assumption
171	% there is a rational map $\mathbb A^n \dashrightarrow V$ with dense image, and the composite
172	% of this with $\alpha$ extends to a morphism $\beta \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \cdots \times
173	% \mathbb P^1 \to A$. An induction argument, starting from Corollary 1.5, shows that there are
174	% regular maps $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$ such that $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum
175	% \beta_i(x_i)$, and the lemma shows that each $\beta_i$ is constant."
176	\begin{proof}
177	  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
178	  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
179	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We isolate the genuine content.
180	
181	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Translating in the group $A$, we may assume $f(\infty) =
182	  0_A$ for a chosen point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. The map $f$ is a morphism out of the
183	  complete nonsingular variety $\mathbb P^1$, so there is no indeterminacy to resolve (a
184	  rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined, Milne
185	  Theorem~3.2; here $f$ is already a morphism).
186	
187	  \textbf{The base case is where the depth lies.} The constancy of $f$ is the statement that
188	  an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$ --- ``$A$ contains no
189	  rational curves''. Over an arbitrary field this does \emph{not} follow from
190	  \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} alone: the Rigidity Lemma collapses a \emph{product}
191	  $X \times Y \to Z$ when one coordinate axis is already collapsed, but for a single map
192	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ no coordinate axis is collapsed a priori (the difference map
193	  $(x, y) \mapsto f(x) - f(y)$ on $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ is constant on an axis only
194	  if $f$ is already constant). The characteristic-free input that supplies the missing fact
195	  is the theorem of the cube (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}): it forces the additive
196	  structure of $\Pic$ and, with it, the triviality of the line bundles a nonconstant
197	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ would produce, so that no such nonconstant morphism exists. (In
198	  characteristic $0$ one may instead pull back an invariant differential and use
199	  $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = 0$; that argument fails in characteristic $p$, which is exactly
200	  why the cube is the committed characteristic-free engine.)
201	
202	  \textbf{Conclusion.} Granting the base case, $f$ is constant with value $f(\infty) = 0_A$
203	  (before the normalising translation, the constant value is the original $f(\infty)$). For
204	  the multi-factor statement of Proposition~3.10 (a general unirational $V$), one writes
205	  $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition (Milne
206	  Corollary~1.5, itself a corollary of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) and applies the base case to
207	  each $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here.
208	\end{proof}
209	
210	\begin{remark}
211	  \label{rmk:cube_is_load_bearing}
212	  The Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) is genuinely used in the surrounding
213	  machinery --- it yields Milne's Corollary~1.5 (additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(- , A)$ over a
214	  product of complete pointed varieties), which drives the multi-factor induction of
215	  Proposition~3.10 and which proves that maps of abelian varieties are homomorphisms up to
216	  translation. But the \emph{single-curve} base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' is
217	  not a formal consequence of the Rigidity Lemma by itself; in characteristic-free form it
218	  rests on \cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}. This refines the iter-156 expectation that a single
219	  $\mathbb P^1$ factor would be cube-free: the multi-factor \emph{induction} is cube-free
220	  (it is pure Rigidity-Lemma additivity), but the base of that induction is not. See the
221	  Strategy-modifying findings of this chapter's writer report.
222	\end{remark}

224	\section{A genus-$0$ curve over $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$}
225	
226	\begin{proposition}
227	[Genus-$0$ curve $\cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$]
228	  \label{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
229	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
230	  \uses{def:genus}
231	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry], Example IV.1.3.5, Ch.~IV \S1 (Riemann--Roch), doc p.~297 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
232	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is rational if it is birational to
233	  % $\mathbf P^1$ (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in this chapter are complete and nonsingular by
234	  % definition, a curve $X$ is rational if and only if $X \cong \mathbf P^1$ (I, 6.12). Now
235	  % using (1.3) we can show that $X$ is rational if and only if $g = 0$. We already know that
236	  % $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely suppose given a curve $X$ of genus 0.
237	  % Let $P, Q$ be two distinct points of $X$ and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor
238	  % $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = -2$, using (1.3.3) above, we have $l(K - D) = 0$, and so
239	  % we find $l(D) = 1$. But $D$ is a divisor of degree 0, so by (1.2) we have $D \sim 0$, in
240	  % other words $P \sim Q$. But this implies $X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
241	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, Algebraic Geometry, Example~IV.1.3.5.}
242	  Let $\bar k$ be algebraically closed and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
243	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
244	\end{proposition}
245	
246	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the statement block above
247	% (Hartshorne states the classification and its proof inside the single Example; the proof is
248	% the second half beginning ``We already know that $p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0$ \ldots'').
249	\begin{proof}
250	  \uses{def:genus}
251	  \emph{Proof sketch (Riemann--Roch level; the formalisation of this step is itself a
252	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
253	
254	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
255	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
256	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
257	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
258	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
259	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
260	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,
261	  i.e.\ $P \sim Q$. A curve possessing two distinct linearly equivalent points is rational
262	  (Hartshorne II.6.10.1: $P \sim Q$ for $P \neq Q$ yields a degree-$1$ map to $\mathbb P^1$),
263	  and a complete nonsingular rational curve is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1$ (Hartshorne I.6.12).
264	  Hence $C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
265	\end{proof}
266	
267	\begin{remark}
268	  \label{rmk:genusZero_iso_subbuild}
269	  \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} is Riemann--Roch--flavoured and its formalisation is a
270	  genuine sub-build, not a short step: Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch theorem for curves, no
271	  divisor class group machinery at this level, and no $\mathbb P^1$-classification of
272	  rational curves. The statement is stated here at textbook level so the chain is complete;
273	  closing its Lean proof will require either importing/porting a substantial slice of curve
274	  theory or re-deriving the genus-$0 \Rightarrow \mathbb P^1$ classification from the
275	  project's own $\genus$ definition. The dependency on $\genus$ (\cref{def:genus}) is the link
276	  to \cref{chap:Genus}.
277	\end{remark}

279	\section{The headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-$0$ curve}
280	
281	\begin{theorem}
282	[Rigidity: a pointed morphism from a genus-$0$ curve to an abelian variety is constant]
283	  \label{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
284	  \label{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}
285	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme}
286	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
287	  Let $\bar k$ be an algebraically closed field (\emph{arbitrary characteristic}; no
288	  $\mathtt{CharZero}$ hypothesis). Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve
289	  over $\bar k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$, and let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
290	  group scheme over $\bar k$ (an abelian variety) with identity $\eta_A$. Then for every
291	  morphism $f \colon C \to A$ such that $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ for some $\bar k$-point
292	  $p \colon \mathbf 1 \to C$, the morphism $f$ equals the constant morphism at the identity,
293	  $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
294	
295	  This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that the genus-$0$
296	  Albanese-witness $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}$ consumes. Its signature mirrors
297	  $\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}
298	  \emph{verbatim except} that the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ instance is dropped: same
299	  curve typeclasses ($\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 1$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$,
300	  $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$ on $C$), same abelian-variety typeclasses
301	  ($\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$
302	  on $A$), and the same pointed-morphism conclusion shape $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi
303	  \eta[A]$.
304	\end{theorem}
305	
306	\begin{proof}
307	  \uses{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant, prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
308	  By \cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1} the genus-$0$ curve $C$ over the algebraically closed
309	  field $\bar k$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via some isomorphism
310	  $\varphi \colon C \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. The composite
311	  $f \circ \varphi^{-1} \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is then a morphism from
312	  $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ to the abelian variety $A$, hence constant by
313	  \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}: its image is a single $\bar k$-point $a_0 \in A$.
314	  Therefore $f$ itself is constant with value $a_0$. The pointed hypothesis
315	  $p \fatsemi f = \eta_A$ evaluates this constant at the $\bar k$-point $p$ and pins it to the
316	  identity, $a_0 = \eta_A$. Consequently $f$ factors through the terminal object as the
317	  constant morphism at the identity, $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \fatsemi \eta_A$.
318	
319	  No differential ($df = 0$) input, no $H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0$ / Serre-duality decomposition,
320	  and no Picard representability is used; the argument is valid in arbitrary characteristic.
321	  Once formalised it lets the $[\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ hypothesis carried by
322	  $\mathtt{rigidity\_over\_kbar}$ be dropped, and (after the $\bar k \to k$ descent step hosted
323	  in \cref{chap:Jacobian}, not here) feeds $\mathtt{genusZeroWitness}.\mathtt{key}$.
324	\end{proof}

10	
11	A self-contained Phase E helper: two morphisms `g₁, g₂ : X ⟶ Y` between
12	schemes over `Spec k` whose restrictions to a non-empty open `U ⊆ X` agree
13	as scheme morphisms — for `X` reduced and geometrically irreducible over
14	`Spec k` and `Y` separated over `Spec k` — agree everywhere. Independent of
15	`AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian C` and provable from current Mathlib alone
16	(Stacks 0BFD plus standard separatedness/irreducibility lemmas).

19	schemes; the formalized statement is the minimal-hypothesis scheme-level
20	form, which the Mumford group-scheme rigidity follows from by specialization.

44	## Hypothesis history
45	
46	### Scheme-level hypothesis (iter 003)
47	
48	The original scaffold from iter 002 had hypothesis
49	`h : ∀ x ∈ U, g₁.left.base x = g₂.left.base x`
50	(point-wise topological equality on `U`). This is too weak: in characteristic
51	`p`, the absolute Frobenius `F : X → X` of an elliptic curve over `𝔽_p` agrees
52	topologically with the identity on all of `X` (the identity on the underlying
53	topological space) but `F ≠ id` as scheme morphisms — they differ on stalks
54	(`a ↦ a` vs `a ↦ a^p`). Hence the symmetric-form rigidity statement
55	`g₁.base = g₂.base on U ⇒ g₁ = g₂` is mathematically false.
56	
57	We strengthen the hypothesis to scheme-level equality on `U`:
58	`h : U.ι ≫ g₁.left = U.ι ≫ g₂.left`. This is the version that is actually
59	needed at the use site (uniqueness half of `exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`):
60	the application produces *scheme-level* agreement on a non-empty open
61	(through the symmetric-power surjection), not merely topological agreement.
62	
63	We also add `[IsReduced X.left]` because Mathlib `b80f227` lacks the lemma
64	`Smooth ⇒ IsReduced` for schemes over a field (the closest is the perfect-base
65	case `Scheme.Hom.dense_smoothLocus_of_perfectField`). This is a small Mathlib
66	gap that the downstream consumer must satisfy via the smoothness + irreducibility
67	of the curve / Jacobian (e.g. via `IsIntegral`).
68	
69	### Unused-hypothesis cleanup (iter 125)
70	
71	Eight decorative hypotheses (`{n m : ℕ}`, `[SmoothOfRelativeDimension n X.hom]`,
72	`[IsProper X.hom]`, `[GrpObj X]`, `[SmoothOfRelativeDimension m Y.hom]`,
73	`[GeometricallyIrreducible Y.hom]`, `[GrpObj Y]`, and the strengthening of
74	`[IsSeparated Y.hom]` to `[IsProper Y.hom]`) were dropped per
75	`analogies/rigidity-refactor.md`. The declaration was also renamed from
76	`GrpObj.eq_of_eqOnOpen` to `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen`, mirroring
77	Mathlib's `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'` naming idiom
78	(`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated:319–322`).
79	-/
80	
81	/-- Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form): two morphisms
82	`g₁, g₂ : X ⟶ Y` between schemes over `Spec k` whose restrictions to a
83	non-empty open `U ⊆ X` agree as scheme morphisms — for `X` reduced and
84	geometrically irreducible over `Spec k` and `Y` separated over `Spec k` —
85	agree everywhere.
86	
87	This is the `Over (Spec (.of k))`-bundled specialization of Mathlib's
88	`ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`. The Mumford group-scheme rigidity
89	statement (a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero) is
90	interderivable with this form using the group law on the target. -/
91	theorem Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen
92	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of k))}
93	    [IsSeparated Y.hom]
94	    [GeometricallyIrreducible X.hom]
95	    [IsReduced X.left]
96	    (g₁ g₂ : X ⟶ Y) (U : X.left.Opens) (hU : (U : Set X.left).Nonempty)
97	    (h : (U.ι : (U : X.left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ X.left) ≫ g₁.left =
98	      (U.ι : (U : X.left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ X.left) ≫ g₂.left) :
99	    g₁ = g₂ := by
100	  -- (1) Provide the separatedness instance under the `OverClass.fromOver`
101	  --     form `Y.left ↘ Spec (.of k)` so that the downstream typeclass
102	  --     search at `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'` finds it.
103	  haveI : IsSeparated (Y.left ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of k)) :=
104	    (‹IsSeparated Y.hom› : IsSeparated Y.hom)
105	  -- (2) `X.left` is irreducible: `Spec k` is a single point (`Unique`,
106	  --     hence `Subsingleton + Nonempty`), and `GeometricallyIrreducible`
107	  --     over a subsingleton base implies `IrreducibleSpace`.
108	  haveI : IrreducibleSpace X.left :=
109	    GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace_of_subsingleton X.hom
110	  -- (3) `U.ι : U.toScheme ⟶ X.left` is dominant: a non-empty open in an
111	  --     irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant.
112	  haveI : IsDominant (U.ι : (U : X.left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ X.left) :=
113	    Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant_ι (IsOpen.dense U.isOpen hU)
114	  -- (4) Promote `g₁ = g₂` from the underlying scheme equality.
115	  --     The `Scheme.Over` and `IsOver` instances for `X.left`, `Y.left`,
116	  --     `g₁.left`, `g₂.left` over `Spec (.of k)` are inferred globally
117	  --     from the `Over (Spec (.of k))` category structure
118	  --     (`OverClass.fromOver`).
119	  refine Over.OverMorphism.ext ?_
120	  exact ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated' (S := Spec (.of k))
121	    (X := X.left) (Y := Y.left) (f := g₁.left) (g := g₂.left) U.ι h
122	
123	end AlgebraicGeometry

/-- **Cartesian-monoidal identity (skeleton step of the Rigidity Lemma).** Post-composing the
second projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y` with the slice section `y ↦ (x₀, y)` is the "collapse the

Pure cartesian-monoidal algebra (no geometry): `comp_lift` distributes the `snd`, the
`𝟙 Y` component simplifies by `Category.comp_id`, and the `toUnit Y` component collapses by
uniqueness of maps into the terminal object. -/
theorem rigidity_snd_lift
    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X) :
    snd X Y ≫ lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) =
      lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) := by
  rw [comp_lift]
  congr 1
  · rw [← Category.assoc]
    congr 1
    exact toUnit_unique _ _
  · simp

/-- **Geometric core of the Rigidity Lemma (honestly deferred).** With `X` complete (proper)
and `x₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ X` a `k̄`-point, the morphism `f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` is invariant under the
"collapse-the-`X`-axis-onto-`x₀`" endomorphism `retract := lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`:

  `f = retract ≫ f`,

i.e. `f(x, y) = f(x₀, y)` for all `(x, y)` — `f` depends only on the `Y`-coordinate. This is
the entirety of the *geometric* content of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma (Form I); the rest of
`rigidity_lemma` is the cartesian-monoidal algebra discharged by `rigidity_snd_lift`.

## Mumford's proof of this core (Abelian Varieties, Ch. II §4, p. 43)

Set `g(y) = f(x₀, y)` (so `retract ≫ f` is exactly `(x, y) ↦ g(y)`). To prove `f = retract ≫ f`
it suffices, since `X ⊗ Y` is a variety (reduced + irreducible) and `Z` is separated, to prove
agreement on a non-empty open `X × V ⊆ X ⊗ Y` (then `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` of

`[IsReduced (X ⊗ Y).left]`, `[IsSeparated Z.hom]`). The open `V` is produced as follows:
let `U` be an affine open neighbourhood of a chosen point in `Z`, `F = Z ∖ U`, and
`G = (snd X Y) '' (f ⁻¹ F)`. Then for each `y ∈ V := Y ∖ G`, the slice `f(X × {y}) ⊆ U`.

This rests on **two Mathlib bridges still to be built** (the cube-free heart):

  1. **Properness ⇒ the projection is a closed map.** `IsProper X.hom` is universally closed,
     and `snd X Y` is the base change of `X.hom` along `Y.hom`, hence universally/closed; so
     `G = snd '' (closed)` is closed and `V` is open. Mathlib has `IsProper`/`UniversallyClosed`

     `Over (Spec k̄)` with the scheme-theoretic pullback projection and transporting
     `IsClosedMap`. (Search: `UniversallyClosed`, `IsProper`, `MorphismProperty.pullback`,

     (`Γ(X) = k̄`), so the image is one point, forcing `f(x, y) = f(x₀, y)`. Mathlib likely lacks
     this in usable form; it is a global-sections-constant argument
     (`H⁰` of a proper geometrically-connected reduced scheme over a field is the field).

These are exactly the "two Mathlib bridges to find/build" flagged in `PROGRESS.md`. Both are
char-free (no theorem of the cube, no cohomology beyond `H⁰`). Until they land, this core is the
sole `sorry` of the Rigidity-Lemma chain. -/

  sorry
